1: <header>LCA of bioenergy chains in Piedmont (Italy): A case study to support public decision makers towards sustainability </header>
G.A. Blengini a,b, E. Brizio' M. Cibrario ³ G. Genon a

<list>a Politecnico di Torino, Corso Duca degli Abruzzi 24, 10129 Turin, Italy 
b CNR-IGAG, Corso Duca degli Abruzzi 24, 10129 Turin, Italy 
C A.R.P.A. Piemonte, Via Vecchia di Borgo 11, 12100 Cuneo, Italy</list>

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 7 July 2011 Received in revised form 25 August 2011 Accepted 6 October 2011 Keywords: LCA Bioenergy Biogas Sustainability Digestate Local emissions|Given the booming of bioenergy plants under construction in Piedmont, in Northern Italy, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) was used in order to assist public decision-makers during the evaluation of new bioenergy projects. Local administrators are in fact worried that public incentives granted to bioen- ergy producers, regardless of the overall environmental performance, might not encourage technological innovation and eco-efficiency, or bring unwanted indirect environmental effects. A detailed LCA of bioen- ergy production from dedicated crops (maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus) and manure through anaerobic digestion and combined heat and power generation was carried out. The LCA model was par- ticularly focused on the end-of-life of digestate and site-specific data related to the impact of adopted energy conversion technologies. It was confirmed that bioenergy is not automatically synonymous with sustainable energy, as the differences in terms of environmental performance can be remarkable. EROI (Energy Return on Investment) index was estimated to be 3-5. The potential in terms of GHG saving depends on several factors and it is heavily influenced by the reference non-renewable energy to be sub- stituted. End-of-life of digestate was found to be crucial for acidification and eutrophication, but also for GHG emissions. Finally, particulates equivalent emissions were found to be very large in comparison to modern natural gas power plants. © 2011 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
2: <header>1. Introduction </header>
Modern bioenergy applications are becoming increasingly important to countries as a low-carbon, distributed, renewable component of national energy sources (Cherubini and Stromman, 2011).
This growing interest in bioenergy is clearly reflected in recent energy policies at EU level, as highlighted in the Directive 2009/28/EC on the promotion of the use of energy from renewable sources.

From the national to the local scales, EU energy policies are enforced through regulations and public incentives schemes, which are mostly aimed at boosting bioenergy generation. However, if on one hand bioenergy chains are deemed to be among the most promising renewable energy alternatives, it must be said that their sustainability is still controversial (Cherubini, 2010; Dickie, 2007; Petrou and Pappis, 2009; Sheehan, 2009). In fact, the conversion of biomass to bioenergy has input and output

flows which may affect its overall environmental performances (Cherubini and Stromman, 2011). Moreover, bioenergy chains are complex and inter-dependent systems made of industrial pro- cesses, which mostly depend on human decisions and control, and agri-forestry subsystems, which man can drive, but cannot control completely, thus complicating the overall picture.

Bioenergy and sustainable energy should not therefore be used as synonymous in energy regulations, as, for instance, conse- quences like land-use change (LUC), N20 soil emissions, change in carbon pools and post-methanation from the storage of digested materials may offset greenhouse gas (GHG) savings. Similarly, pub- lic incentives might distort energy markets, or create unfair and inefficient competition against food and feed crops.

For this reasons, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) is increasingly been adopted as an analytical tool that is able to capture complexity and inter-dependencies, thus providing a comprehensive and objective environmental balance, helpful to address sustainability of bioen- ergy chains.

A recent, fairly comprehensive and extensive literature review by Cherubini and Stromman (2011) pointed out how LCA of bioenergy systems can be helpful to address sustainability, but, at the same time, also showed how methodological assumptions might distort the results or render comparisons nearly impossible.

Similarly to LCA of agri-food systems, harmonisation and sector- specific methodologies are necessary (Audsley et al., 1997; Blengini and Busto, 2009; Schlamadinger et al., 1997; Williams et al., 2006).

In particular, in order to develop meaningful LCAs of bioenergy systems: (1) goal and scope must be clearly identified, justified and outlined; (2) both input data and inventory results must be fully made available and it should be possible to mathematically manipulate them; (3) bioenergy LCAs should necessarily reflect the complex combination of the territorial context, including site- specific climate conditions, the local agri-forestry practices, the adopted energy conversion technologies and, finally, the disposal of residues.

Bearing this in mind, this paper presents a LCA case study focused on bioenergy production from dedicated crops (maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus) and manure through anaerobic digestion (AD) and combined heat and power CHP generation.

Given the booming of bioenergy plants under construction in Piedmont, in Northern Italy, a LCA was conducted in order to assist public decision-makers during the evaluation of new bioen- ergy projects. Local administrators are in fact worried that public incentives granted to bioenergy producers, regardless of the over- all environmental performance, might not encourage technological innovation and eco-efficiency, or bring unwanted indirect environ- mental effects.
Based on the state-of-art of bioenergy LCAs reported in Cherubini and Stromman (2011), the original contribution of the present paper can be summarised as follows:

<list>to the authors' knowledge there are very few LCAs of bioen- ergy systems in Italy (Corti and Lombardi, 2004) and none that can provide a comprehensive picture of bioenergy generation via dedicated crops, subsequent AD and CHP conversion; 
the LCA model includes the whole bioenergy chain, with partic- ular attention paid to the end-of-life (EOL) of digestate, which is often disregarded or underestimated in previous LCAs; 
local air emissions from the foreground system are carefully con- sidered and reported, based on average measured data, while in several LCA studies only GHG are accounted for; 
the proposed LCA model reflects site-specific data and uncer- tainties relevant to different technologies, according to different mixes of energy crops and manure.</list>
The above elements of novelty are framed within the interna- tional literature as it is highlighted in the next section.

6: <header>2. Literature review </header>

In the literature there are many references about the arguments of bioenergy production and connected environmental sustainabil- ity, and to these aims it has been studied the individuation and utilisation of indicators or methodologies corresponding to LCA; the evaluated aspects concern the definition of the fundamental parameters for the evaluation of the specific inventories (agri- cultural activity, energetic production, transport, management of residuals), their application to specific case studies, the comparison of energetic production by renewable systems with conventional ones.

From the general field of bioenergy production, with different solutions to extract energy from biomass, in particular we consid- ered the development of biogas system, in order to define the basis for our specific case evaluation.

To these aims, and with emphasis on biogas production and utilisation, in Pöschl et al. (2010) the energy efficiency of different biogas systems was evaluated and specific energy bal- ances were defined; the study provides bases for assessment of

environmental compatibility, including management of digestate. It has been observed (Börjesson and Berglund, 2007) that bio- gas systems lead to environmental improvements, arising from changed land use and handling of organic waste products, which often exceed the direct benefits from fossil fuel replacement; from the other side an impact factor, of different numerical values, can be originated, arising from the utilised raw mate- rial, the energy service that is provided, the replaced reference system.

The very important aspect of digestate characteristics, the possi- ble use as fertiliser and the potential impacts from the point of view of volatilisation have been extensively studied by Bernstad and la Cour Jansen (2011). With reference to the organic fraction of munic- ipal solid waste, the possibility to replace conventional fertilisers with fermentation residues has been studied by Svensson et al. (2004). The potential emissions from soils fertilised with digestate have been investigated by Bouwman et al. (2002).

The use of LCA has been suggested by Jury et al. (2010) to eval- uate the contribution to climate change of biogas production by monofermentation of cultivated crops, and it resulted absolutely lower than the contribution of natural gas importation; also the effects on ecosystem quality and human health damages were eval- uated.

In order to define the required information concerning energetic aspects, experiences of co-digestion of energy crops and cow or pig manures have been conducted on different scales (Lehtomäki et al., 2007; Panichnumsin et al., 2010), in order to define the influence of operating parameters on methane yield and post-methanation potential.

From a methodological point of view, as concerns the for- mal structure of LCA, a standard methodology has been outlined (Schlamadinger et al., 1997) for comparing the greenhouse gas bal- ances of bioenergy systems with those of fossil energy systems: a careful definition of system boundaries, and many operating issues have been dealt with in detail, with the final aim of an optimi- sation from the greenhouse gas emissions point of view. In order to establish a more reliable approach to the impact assessment of biomass cultivation phase, a new LCA methodology, tailored to biomass production, was developed by Buratti and Fantozzi (2010), and data from experimental fields were used for testing. The aspect of GHG balances of bioenergy systems producing electricity, heat and transportation biofuels has been examined in comparison with fossil reference systems in Cherubini (2010), proceeding from stan- dard LCA.

With reference to a small scale plant directed to co-generate electricity and district heating from biomass, environmental bur- dens for different times were identified with a life cycle inventory (Jungmeier et al., 1998); the objective of the work was also to identify possibilities for reduction of burdens and perspectives for research and development.

The LCA methodology has also been applied (Hanegraaf et al., 1998) to the evaluation of the ecological and socio-economic sus- tainability of biomass crops: for different energy crops a net energy budget has been defined, with reference to avoided fossil energy, and it was possible to establish that, for large scale use, energy poli- cies are required and policy instruments for financial incentives could be a preferred tool.

The differences in LCA elaborations concerning environmen- tal and energy performances of bioenergy have been examined in Cherubini et al. (2009), and the influence on the final results has been discussed with emphasis on many operational and impact evaluation aspects; also if many key issues are site-specific, and consequently it is impossible to generalise GHG emissions and fossil fuel savings, some recommendations and emerging implications about the future trends of bioenergy are pointed out.

Many specific applications to regional or local energy produc- tion strategies have been described in case studies, normally by employing the instruments of energy and mass balances and LCA: the environmental performance of willow biomass crop production systems in New York has been analysed (Heller et al., 2003), lead- ing to the assertion of sustainability and environmental benefits; an evaluation of the potential for regional bioenergy co-generative recovery by small-scale fermentation systems has been investi- gated in the area of the Tokyo Bay, and some results concerning potential electricity production and reduction of CO2 emissions from fossil fuels have been determined (Wong et al., 2008); the feasibility of a socio-economic and environmental impact analysis for large-scale bioenergy production for the La Pampa province in Argentina has been analysed (van Dam et al., 2009), with the con- clusion that it is difficult to give ex ante general indications about bioenergy chain sustainability and its dependence on operating parameters and land use system.

From the indicated references it is possible to establish that the environmental balances for energy crops exploitation are well defined and many examples of application are at disposal for useful comparisons; in any case a specific definition of the local context and the existing operating conditions must be carefully examined, in order to arrive to valid conclusions for a proposed application.

The LCA methodology has also been applied (Hanegraaf et al., 1998) to the evaluation of the ecological and socio-economic sus- tainability of biomass crops: for different energy crops a net energy budget has been defined, with reference to avoided fossil energy, and it was possible to establish that, for large scale use, energy poli- cies are required and policy instruments for financial incentives could be a preferred tool.

The differences in LCA elaborations concerning environmen- tal and energy performances of bioenergy have been examined in Cherubini et al. (2009), and the influence on the final results has been discussed with emphasis on many operational and impact evaluation aspects; also if many key issues are site-specific, and consequently it is impossible to generalise GHG emissions and fossil fuel savings, some recommendations and emerging implications about the future trends of bioenergy are pointed out.

Many specific applications to regional or local energy produc- tion strategies have been described in case studies, normally by employing the instruments of energy and mass balances and LCA: the environmental performance of willow biomass crop production systems in New York has been analysed (Heller et al., 2003), lead- ing to the assertion of sustainability and environmental benefits; an evaluation of the potential for regional bioenergy co-generative recovery by small-scale fermentation systems has been investi- gated in the area of the Tokyo Bay, and some results concerning potential electricity production and reduction of CO2 emissions from fossil fuels have been determined (Wong et al., 2008); the feasibility of a socio-economic and environmental impact analysis for large-scale bioenergy production for the La Pampa province in Argentina has been analysed (van Dam et al., 2009), with the con- clusion that it is difficult to give ex ante general indications about bioenergy chain sustainability and its dependence on operating parameters and land use system.

From the indicated references it is possible to establish that the environmental balances for energy crops exploitation are well defined and many examples of application are at disposal for useful comparisons; in any case a specific definition of the local context and the existing operating conditions must be carefully examined, in order to arrive to valid conclusions for a proposed application.

13: <header>3. Materials and methods </header>
3.1. Energy crops and bioenergy chains

Four dedicated energy crops cultivated in Northern Italy are considered: maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus. Once har- vested, crops are co-digested in mix with cow manure and the biogas obtained is burnt into an internal combustion heat and power generator. Digestate is then stored and subsequently spread on cropland.

These bioenergy chains, which differ to each other only in the agricultural section, reflect a typical operating condition in the study area: Piedmont Region in Northern Italy. They were chosen to be representative of the new bioenergy projects under construc- tion or authorisation. The study therefore reflects average present and future bioenergy production in the study area.
14: <header>3.2. LCA modelling and key methodological assumptions </header>
From-cradle-to-grave environmental implications of the four bioenergy chains were investigated through LCA according to the standard ISO 14040 (2006).

Authors of this paper assume that the reader already has a basic knowledge of LCA and bioenergy production, so that gen- eral information on these aspects is not provided here. Only key methodological assumptions are therefore presented.
System boundaries are those described in Fig. 1, where three main subsystems are emphasised:

<list>Agricultural processes (encompassing all direct and auxiliary agri- cultural activities ending with harvesting and transportation of the energy crop); 
Energy conversion (encompassing mixing of energy crop and manure, anaerobic co-digestion, biogas production and CHP gen- eration); 
End-of-life (EOL) of digestate (encompassing storing and spreading of the digestate).</list>

Functional unit (FU) is 1 MJ of net energy delivered (electricity or heat) and allocation of environmental impacts between electric- ity and heat is based on energy content. According to Cherubini and

Stromman (2011) there is not uniformity concerning the functional unit and the allocation method chosen by the different analysts, and this makes LCA results of difficult comparison. According to the same authors, FU should also be expressed per unit of cropland, as this is an extremely important parameter since biomass can com- pete against food, feed or fibre production under land-availability constraints. Further attention to this issue will be paid in the dis- cussion section, where data related to the use of cropland will be provided in order to enable the reader to change the FU.

Moreover, the consequence of an allocation method based on energy content is that the environmental impacts associated with 1 MJ of electricity and MJ of heat are the same. An allocation method based on exergy, or market value, or exclusion of heat, would increase the environmental impacts associated with elec- tricity. For these reasons, the results will be provided with enough detail in order to enable the reader to change the allocation method.

Data used for LCA modelling and their sources are described in the next chapter or reported as electronic supplementary material. Where not otherwise specified, the reference database for sec- ondary inventory data is Ecoinvent (2007).
The LCA model was created with the aid of the SimaPro 7 software application (2006).
System expansion is adopted in order to account for avoided production of chemical fertilisers consequent to spreading of diges- tate on cropland, as it will be described in the next section.

As far as selection of impact indicators is concerned, these were chosen to be representative of broadly recognized areas of environ- mental concern, according to the ISO recommendations (ISO 14040, 2006).
Selected indicators are the following:

<list>GER (Gross Energy Requirement) expressing the primary energy resource consumption: direct + indirect + feedstock (Boustead and Hancock, 1979); 
- NRE (Non Renewable Energy) as the non-renewable part of GER; 
- GWP (Global Warming Potential - 100 years), as an indicator of the greenhouse effect; 
- AP (Acidification Potential), as an indicator of acid rain phe- nomenon; 
- EP (Eutrophication Potential), as an indicator of surface water eutrophication; 
- POCP (Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential), as an indicator of photo-smog creation.</list>

Characterisation factors for GWP, AP, EP and POCP are reported in the Method "CML, 2 baseline 2000" (SimaPro7, 2006). Among these, GWP of methane is assumed to be 23 kg and nitrous oxide 296 kg CO2 Carbon uptake during plant growth and biogenic carbon dioxide emissions along the whole bioenergy chain were accounted for and reported. Since the greenhouse effect is determined either by fossil or by biogenic carbon dioxide (Blengini, 2008; Hogg et al., 2008), in LCA of bioenergy the assumption that the biogenic carbon cycle is neutral (the amount of CO2 absorbed during plant growth is equiv- alent to greenhouse emissions released during energy conversion and end-of-life of residues) is often an oversimplification. Due to the fact that some local and regional scale environmental impacts are not fully captured by the selected indicators, and bear- ing in mind that the problem of secondary fine dust is felt of sanitary concern by the local public authorities, some selected air emissions, thought as precursors of fine dust, will be reported separately: NOx, NH3, Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC). As a matter of fact, air quality in Northern Italy is one of the most polluted of the world, maybe the worst in Europe, due to the intense human activities and the orography of its territory. PM10 NO2 and ozone concentrations measured at the ground level

diffusely and permanently go beyond the quality standards (EEA, 2010). In particular, PM10 concentration is only partly due to par- ticulate primary emissions because the chemical analysis of PM measured in Northern Italy confirm that secondary particles (deriv- ing from NOx, SOx, NH3 and VOC) account for 60-70% of total PM concentration. Moreover, some European studies (de Leeuw, 2002) report the following aerosol formation factors, to be considered by weight, starting from gaseous pollutants: NOx 0.88; SOx 0.54; NH3 0.64; VOC 0.02. As it is clear from the reported figures, in order to control and improve air quality in Northern Italy, the emissions of gaseous compounds, especially NOx and ammonia (mostly emitted by agriculture), should be reduced.

Since the greenhouse effect is determined either by fossil or by biogenic carbon dioxide (Blengini, 2008; Hogg et al., 2008), in LCA of bioenergy the assumption that the biogenic carbon cycle is neutral (the amount of CO2 absorbed during plant growth is equiv- alent to greenhouse emissions released during energy conversion and end-of-life of residues) is often an oversimplification. Due to the fact that some local and regional scale environmental impacts are not fully captured by the selected indicators, and bear- ing in mind that the problem of secondary fine dust is felt of sanitary concern by the local public authorities, some selected air emissions, thought as precursors of fine dust, will be reported separately: NOx, NH3, Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC). As a matter of fact, air quality in Northern Italy is one of the most polluted of the world, maybe the worst in Europe, due to the intense human activities and the orography of its territory. PM10 NO2 and ozone concentrations measured at the ground level

diffusely and permanently go beyond the quality standards (EEA, 2010). In particular, PM10 concentration is only partly due to par- ticulate primary emissions because the chemical analysis of PM measured in Northern Italy confirm that secondary particles (deriv- ing from NOx, SOx, NH3 and VOC) account for 60-70% of total PM concentration. Moreover, some European studies (de Leeuw, 2002) report the following aerosol formation factors, to be considered by weight, starting from gaseous pollutants: NOx 0.88; SOx 0.54; NH3 0.64; VOC 0.02. As it is clear from the reported figures, in order to control and improve air quality in Northern Italy, the emissions of gaseous compounds, especially NOx and ammonia (mostly emitted by agriculture), should be reduced.
16: <header>3.2. LCA modelling and key methodological assumptions </header>
From-cradle-to-grave environmental implications of the four bioenergy chains were investigated through LCA according to the standard ISO 14040 (2006).

Authors of this paper assume that the reader already has a basic knowledge of LCA and bioenergy production, so that gen- eral information on these aspects is not provided here. Only key methodological assumptions are therefore presented.
System boundaries are those described in Fig. 1, where three main subsystems are emphasised:

<list>Agricultural processes (encompassing all direct and auxiliary agri- cultural activities ending with harvesting and transportation of the energy crop); 
Energy conversion (encompassing mixing of energy crop and manure, anaerobic co-digestion, biogas production and CHP gen- eration); 
End-of-life (EOL) of digestate (encompassing storing and spreading of the digestate).</list>

Functional unit (FU) is 1 MJ of net energy delivered (electricity or heat) and allocation of environmental impacts between electric- ity and heat is based on energy content. According to Cherubini and

Stromman (2011) there is not uniformity concerning the functional unit and the allocation method chosen by the different analysts, and this makes LCA results of difficult comparison. According to the same authors, FU should also be expressed per unit of cropland, as this is an extremely important parameter since biomass can com- pete against food, feed or fibre production under land-availability constraints. Further attention to this issue will be paid in the dis- cussion section, where data related to the use of cropland will be provided in order to enable the reader to change the FU.

Moreover, the consequence of an allocation method based on energy content is that the environmental impacts associated with 1 MJ of electricity and MJ of heat are the same. An allocation method based on exergy, or market value, or exclusion of heat, would increase the environmental impacts associated with elec- tricity. For these reasons, the results will be provided with enough detail in order to enable the reader to change the allocation method.

Data used for LCA modelling and their sources are described in the next chapter or reported as electronic supplementary material. Where not otherwise specified, the reference database for sec- ondary inventory data is Ecoinvent (2007).
The LCA model was created with the aid of the SimaPro 7 software application (2006).
System expansion is adopted in order to account for avoided production of chemical fertilisers consequent to spreading of diges- tate on cropland, as it will be described in the next section.

As far as selection of impact indicators is concerned, these were chosen to be representative of broadly recognized areas of environ- mental concern, according to the ISO recommendations (ISO 14040, 2006).

<list>GER (Gross Energy Requirement) expressing the primary energy resource consumption: direct + indirect + feedstock (Boustead and Hancock, 1979); 
- NRE (Non Renewable Energy) as the non-renewable part of GER; 
- GWP (Global Warming Potential - 100 years), as an indicator of the greenhouse effect; 
- AP (Acidification Potential), as an indicator of acid rain phe- nomenon; 
- EP (Eutrophication Potential), as an indicator of surface water eutrophication; 
- POCP (Photochemical Ozone Creation Potential), as an indicator of photo-smog creation.</list>

Characterisation factors for GWP, AP, EP and POCP are reported in the Method "CML, 2 baseline 2000" (SimaPro7, 2006). Among these, GWP of methane is assumed to be 23 kg and nitrous oxide 296 kg CO2 Carbon uptake during plant growth and biogenic carbon dioxide emissions along the whole bioenergy chain were accounted for and reported. Since the greenhouse effect is determined either by fossil or by biogenic carbon dioxide (Blengini, 2008; Hogg et al., 2008), in LCA of bioenergy the assumption that the biogenic carbon cycle is neutral (the amount of CO2 absorbed during plant growth is equiv- alent to greenhouse emissions released during energy conversion and end-of-life of residues) is often an oversimplification. Due to the fact that some local and regional scale environmental impacts are not fully captured by the selected indicators, and bear- ing in mind that the problem of secondary fine dust is felt of sanitary concern by the local public authorities, some selected air emissions, thought as precursors of fine dust, will be reported separately: NOx, NH3, Volatile Organic Compounds (VOC). As a matter of fact, air quality in Northern Italy is one of the most polluted of the world, maybe the worst in Europe, due to the intense human activities and the orography of its territory. PM10 NO2 and ozone concentrations measured at the ground level

diffusely and permanently go beyond the quality standards (EEA, 2010). In particular, PM10 concentration is only partly due to par- ticulate primary emissions because the chemical analysis of PM measured in Northern Italy confirm that secondary particles (deriv- ing from NOx, SOx, NH3 and VOC) account for 60-70% of total PM concentration. Moreover, some European studies (de Leeuw, 2002) report the following aerosol formation factors, to be considered by weight, starting from gaseous pollutants: NOx 0.88; SOx 0.54; NH3 0.64; VOC 0.02. As it is clear from the reported figures, in order to control and improve air quality in Northern Italy, the emissions of gaseous compounds, especially NOx and ammonia (mostly emitted by agriculture), should be reduced.

19: <header>4. Inventory analysis of the four bioenergy chains </header>
The following paragraphs presents data collection and subse- quent implementation in the LCA model. In order to reduce the paper lengths, electronic supplementary material is used where necessary.
20: All agricultural processes are assumed to take place in Pied- mont (North-West Italy), therefore all factors depending on climate conditions or agricultural soil characteristics reflect the study area.

Three out of four biomasses are annual crops: the bio-cycle of maize and sorghum covers the period end of March to beginning of September, while triticale cultivation begins in October and ends in June. The bio-cycle of the fourth biomass (miscanthus) is extended over 20 years, of which only 18 can be exploited for bioenergy pro- duction.

The following paragraphs show inventory data relevant to the agricultural subsystem, starting from agricultural soil preparation and ending with the transportation of the harvested crop to the digester. A flow chart of the agricultural subsystem is reported as electronic supplementary material.

With reference to the agricultural phase, inventory data are expressed per unit of cropland (ha) and subsequently implemented in the LCA model using the average biomass harvesting yields (t/ha, year) reported in Table 1 (LIFE, 2009; Monteleone, 2008). Data rel- evant to miscanthus are collected with reference to the average
Table 1 Average yearly harvesting yields in the study area (fresh matter).
Bionergy crop|Harvested biomass (t/hay-1) Maize|70 Sorghum|75 Triticale|40 Miscanthus|22.5

20 year lifetime, keeping in mind that the overall biomass produc- tion is 450t/ha (from year 3 to year 20), therefore yielding a yearly average of 22.5t/ha in the total biomass cycle. Consequently, all miscanthus' data were adjusted according to the 20 year bio-cycle and reported as yearly average.
21: All agricultural processes are assumed to take place in Pied- mont (North-West Italy), therefore all factors depending on climate conditions or agricultural soil characteristics reflect the study area.

Three out of four biomasses are annual crops: the bio-cycle of maize and sorghum covers the period end of March to beginning of September, while triticale cultivation begins in October and ends in June. With reference to the agricultural phase, inventory data are expressed per unit of cropland (ha) and subsequently implemented in the LCA model using the average biomass harvesting yields (t/ha, year) reported in Table 1 (LIFE, 2009; Monteleone, 2008). Data rel- evant to miscanthus are collected with reference to the average

Bionergy crop|Harvested biomass (t/hay-1) Maize|70 Sorghum|75 Triticale|40 Miscanthus|22.5

20 year lifetime, keeping in mind that the overall biomass produc- tion is 450t/ha (from year 3 to year 20), therefore yielding a yearly average of 22.5t/ha in the total biomass cycle. Consequently, all miscanthus' data were adjusted according to the 20 year bio-cycle and reported as yearly average.
22: Field operations encompass shallow ploughing, in case of maize and triticale, deep ploughing, for sorghum and miscanthus, and harrowing, for all crops. Yearly diesel consumption was estimated according to the literature data (Anonymous, 2007a; Williams et al., 2006): 261/ha for maize and triticale and 331/ha for sorghum and miscanthus. In the case of miscanthus, field operations in prepara- tion of seeding occur only once in the 20 year bio-cycle, therefore diesel use was adjusted accordingly.
23: Average use of chemical fertilisers was estimated through the literature data (Anonymous, 2007b; Regione Lombardia, 2007; Williams et al., 2006), while the nutrient contribution of crop residues from previous farming was neglected in order to be con- servative. Table 2 shows average yearly quantities of fertilisers expressed as nutrient content. Average transportation distance for fertilisers is 20 km. A diesel consumption of 7/ha was considered for fertilisers spreading according to Williams et al. (2006).
24: Seeding and subsequent agricultural operations correspond to the following consumption of diesel, according to the literature data (Ecoinvent, 2007; Regione Lombardia, 2007; Williams et al., 2006): 111/ha for maize, sorghum and triticale and 7.51/ha for mis- canthus. As miscanthus is concerned, while seeding takes place

26: As use of pesticide is quite variable from farm to farm, aver- age data were retrieved from the literature and sector-specific studies (Anonymous, 2007a; Ecoinvent, 2007; Regione Lombardia, 2007; Williams et al., 2006). Commercial pesticides were mod- elled according to the active ingredients and according to inventory data from Ecoinvent. In some cases, no precise match was found and an active ingredient of similar chemical class was adopted instead. Average transportation distance for pesticides is 20 km. A diesel consumption of 31/ha was considered for pesticides spread- ing (601/ha for miscanthus). Data relevant to pesticides are reported in Table 3.
27: Average water consumption has been estimated through the literature data (Anonymous, 2007a,b; Regione Lombardia, 2007), taking into account current farming practices, site specific charac- teristics of agricultural soils, evapo-transpiration, biomass uptake and, finally, the contribution of rainfall during the irrigation period (200 m³ /ha). A mix of furrow (80%) and sprinkler (20%) irrigation was considered, which reflects average current irrigation prac- tices. The following yearly quantities of water were considered in the LCA model: maize 2600 /ha; sorghum 1470 /ha; trit- icale 600 m³ /ha; miscanthus Using the literature data (Busato et al., 2009), the following electricity and diesel con- sumptions were considered in the model: maize 286 kWh/ha and 8.31/ha; sorghum 162 kWh/ha and 4.71/ha; triticale 66 kWh/ha and 21/ha; miscanthus 640 kWh/ha and 191/ha.
28: Energy use for the harvesting process and subsequent tempo- rary storage was included in the model according to the literature data (Anonymous, 2007a; Ecoinvent, 2007; Regione Lombardia, 2007; Williams et al., 2006): 43.61/ha of diesel and 250 kWh/ha of electricity for all the energy crops. Truck transportation towards the co-generator over an average distance of 70 km was considered as well.
29: Land use (LU) and land use change (LUC) were modelled accord- ing to the site-specific conditions, local land management practices and the goal and scope of the present study.

As an assumption supported by the current practices, bioenergy crops are cultivated in areas that were already used for agricultural purposes with similar land management, therefore LUC issues and change in carbon pools are excluded from the LCA. Indirect LUC, which occurs when land currently used for other purposes is con- verted into bioenergy feedstock production and the demand for the previous land use remains, is excluded. The latter assumption,

which reflects the short/medium term local situation, where the previous agricultural production is in fact not replaced, corresponds to an attributional LCA (Finnveden et al., 2009). This is helpful to depict the present framework.

In a future study, which is currently in progress, direct and indirect LUC issues will be re-considered in a broader context of consequential LCA modelling (Finnveden et al., 2009), which is regarded as more relevant for long-term decision making in the field of bioenergy (Cherubini and Stromman, 2011).
Bearing these remarks in mind, occupation of arable land was considered in the model, according to the quantities that can easily be calculated through data reported in Table 1.

The CO2 uptake was calculated according to the quantity of car- bon sequestered from the atmosphere during the biomass growth, under the assumption that 45% of the dry matter is carbon (LIFE, 2009). Accordingly, 500 kg/t of carbon dioxide sequestered from air in case of harvested maize, sorghum and triticale, and 820 kg/t for miscanthus, were used in the model. The biomass feedstock energy was estimated using the Gross Calorific Value of the dry matter and the water content: maize 6000 MJ/t; sorghum 5670 MJ/t; triticale 5700 MJ/t; miscanthus 8550 MJ/t.

The biomass feedstock energy was estimated using the Gross Calorific Value of the dry matter and the water content: maize 6000 MJ/t; sorghum 5670 MJ/t; triticale 5700 MJ/t; miscanthus 8550 MJ/t.
31: LCA practitioners usually refer production equipment and facilities as "capital goods" or "capital equipment". The indirect environmental burdens caused by the manufacturing, maintenance and disposal of capital goods (machinery) used in the agricultural phase were estimated using inventory data from Ecoinvent. Data relevant to capital goods are reported as electronic supplementary material. Average local farming practices and the literature data (Audsley et al., 1997; Maraseni et al., 2007; Williams et al., 2006) were considered in order to allocate impacts according to the total harvested biomass during the expected capital good lifetime.
32: Bio-chemical field emissions encompass air, water and soil emissions due to cultivation practices. Air and water emissions related to the use of fertilisers encompass: N20 and NO due to de-nitrification; NH3 due to volatilisation; NO3 due to leaching or runoff; PO43- due to leaching or runoff.

Bearing this in mind, air and water emissions used in this study are reported in Table 4, which is based on the literature data (Anonymous, 2007a; Audsley et al., 1997; Ecoinvent, 2007; Masoni and Pampana, 2003; Williams et al., 2006).

Soil emissions reported in Table 5 are mostly a consequence of pesticides and chemical fertilisers use (Ecoinvent, 2007). Moreover, the uptake of heavy metals from soils during the plant growth was not considered, as the digestate is supposed to be returned to the same cropland.
33: The second step in the bioenergy chain includes co-digestion and subsequent combustion of the biogas finalised to heat and electricity generation (Fig. 2). Harvested crops are mixed with cow

estimated to be 60 days, which is considered enough in order to obtain a satisfactory volatile solids conversion rate. Quantities of produced biogas and its methane content are estimated using the literature data (Piccinini, 2003) according to the raw mix compo- sition (Table 6) and the characteristics of substrates, as shown in Table 7.

As an assumption suggested by the current state-of-art in the study area, and according to the average data recorded by the local air quality control authorities, it was estimated that 5% of generated biogas is lost due to leakages in the collection and pressure regu- lating system. According to Börjesson and Berglund (2007), these losses are reported normally to correspond to less than 2%, but may vary between 0.2% and 11-13%. Therefore, this fugitive emission of methane is subtracted from the biogas flow that is fed into the co- generator (Table 8). Such an assumption will be further highlighted and commented in Section 5.
As it can be seen in Table 8, the quantity of produced biogas increases with the increase in energy crop content (scenarios 1-3) and is maximum for miscanthus.

7: <header>4.2.2. Co-generation </header>

Biogas undergoes a de-sulfurisation treatment prior to be burnt in a CHP internal combustion engine. Assuming an average LHV of the biogas of 19MJ/Nm³, an electricity generation efficiency of 40% and a heat generation efficiency of 40%, and assuming that 50% of the heat produced is internally used to heat the digesters, the obtained net electricity and heat are shown in Table 8. Electricity is sold and delivered to the grid, while net heat is used in local farming and/or agri-food activities or for household purposes.

Air emissions from the co-generation unit are reported in Table 9, both for emission limits imposed by Italian legislation and emission levels that can be measured at the stack. It should be remembered that, generally, biogas engines comply with NOx limits (500 mg/Nm3, dry gas and O2 at 5%) by means of a lean com- bustion (that is strong excess air). Besides the described primary measures aiming at reducing NOx emissions from biogas engine, some secondary devices could be applied at the end of pipe, the selective catalytic reduction (SCR) in particular. The mentioned cat- alytic technology is surely available and economically sustainable; some installations are already running in Northern Italy and this
device represents the so-called Best Available Technique for future applications.

An analogous reasoning can be developed for VOC emissions: in this case, Italian laws impose an emission limit of 150 mg/Nm³ (dry gas, O2 at 5%) for the parameter TOC (total organic carbon), that is carbon coming from both methanic and non methanic compounds. Existing biogas engines are normally equipped with catalytic oxidizers (CATOX) applied in order to reduce CO and unburned hydrocarbons. Methane slippage coming from the engine (hundreds of mg/Nm³ can be decreased by means of a thermal oxidizer (THERMOX) where a small fraction of biogas is burned to sustain the reactions; also in this case THERMOX is considered the Best Available Technique for the next biogas plants.

As a consequence of the reported configurations, emission lev- els of NO and CH4 shown in Table 9 express two potential values for NOx (with or without SCR reactor) and the expected range for methane slippage that could be very large, as previously mentioned. The other reported emission levels reflect the currently adopted technologies in the study area and the average emissions recorded by the local air quality control authorities.

10: <header>4.3. Digestate management </header>

As a current practice in the study area, digestate is transferred, without dewatering, into a storage pond for an average period of 180 days and subsequently spread over cropland in partial substi- tution of chemical fertilisers.

Pollutant emissions during storage and spreading were esti- mated using the literature data (Amon et al., 2006; EEA, 2005; IPCC, 2006). In order to calculate emissions reported in Table 10, the following parameters had to be assumed.
11: <header>Table 10 </header>
Emission factors from digestate storage and spreading.

|Emission factor NH3-N storage (% of N storage)|19 NH3-N spreading (% of N storage)|15 NOx-N (kg NOx-N/kg N)|0.00054 N2O-N direct (kg N2O-N/kg N)|0.0032 N2O-N indirect (kg ;N2O-N/kgNH3-N+NOx-Nvolatilised)|0.01
Nitrogen content in the digestate was assumed to be 4.3 kg of N per tonne of harvested crop and 3 kg of N per tonne of fresh manure fed into the co-digester (Brizio and Genon, 2009).

According to the average operational co-digestion conditions, mainly temperature and retention time, VS removal during AD was assumed to be 50%. The post-methanation potential is assumed to be 501 CH4/kg of the residual VS (Brizio and Genon, 2009). Transport-related impacts over a distance of 70 km by truck were also included.

Based on reliable emission factors and international studies (Brizio and Genon, 2009; Clemens et al., 2006; EEA, 2005; IPCC, 2006), which mainly refer to fresh animal manure, it is possible to assume that 34 11% of nitrogen contained in the storage is emit- ted as NH3-N from the storage and land-spreading (Table 10). It is important to underline that the reported uncontrolled ammo- nia emissions refer to uncovered storage tank and traditional land-spreading techniques, thus they could be strongly reduced by applying Best Available Techniques (European IPPC Bureau, 2003).

Table 10 also reports emissions of N20 from storage of fresh manure, indirect N20 emissions due to volatilised nitrogen and NOx emissions. We do not have experimental information about the effect of increased nitrogen content due to co-digestion of manure and energy crops but, according to the approach followed by inter- national emission factors (N20 releases are likely to be proportional to nitrogen content), a further emissive increase for N20 could be expected in this case, so reported N20 emissions could be even underestimated.

Nonetheless, spreading of digestate on cropland corresponds to the substitution of chemical fertilisers in quantities that depend on the characteristics of the digestate itself and a coefficient of utilisa- tion of its NPK content. Substitution of chemical fertilisers avoids production and transport-related impacts and also bio-chemical emissions due to their use.

As far as N-fertiliser is concerned, once N-based emission from storage and spreading are accounted for (Table 10), it is assumed that 75% (Masoni and Pampana, 2003; Williams et al., 2006) of the nitrogen remaining after storage can substitute ammonium nitrate. The remaining nitrogen quota is emitted towards the ground water (as nitrates), increasing nitrate contamination of surface and ground-water that represents another environmental critical issue in Northern Italy.

Based on the literature data (Jury et al., 2010; Lukehurst et al., 2010), it was assumed that P2O5 and K20 content in the harvested energy crop is, respectively, 0.5 kg/t and 5 kg/t. Similarly, P2O5 and K20 content in fresh manure were assumed to be, respectively, 3 kg/t and 8 kg/t. The coefficients of substitution of P2O5 and K2O in terms of single superphosphate and potassium chloride were assumed to be 75% of the nutrient content in the digestate (Jury et al., 2010).
12: <header>Table 10 </header>
Emission factors from digestate storage and spreading.

|Emission factor NH3-N storage (% of N storage)|19 NH3-N spreading (% of N storage)|15 NOx-N (kg NOx-N/kg N)|0.00054 N2O-N direct (kg N2O-N/kg N)|0.0032 N2O-N indirect (kg ;N2O-N/kgNH3-N+NOx-Nvolatilised)|0.01
Nitrogen content in the digestate was assumed to be 4.3 kg of N per tonne of harvested crop and 3 kg of N per tonne of fresh manure fed into the co-digester (Brizio and Genon, 2009).

According to the average operational co-digestion conditions, mainly temperature and retention time, VS removal during AD was assumed to be 50%. The post-methanation potential is assumed to be 501 CH4/kg of the residual VS (Brizio and Genon, 2009). Transport-related impacts over a distance of 70 km by truck were also included.

Based on reliable emission factors and international studies (Brizio and Genon, 2009; Clemens et al., 2006; EEA, 2005; IPCC, 2006), which mainly refer to fresh animal manure, it is possible to assume that 34 11% of nitrogen contained in the storage is emit- ted as NH3-N from the storage and land-spreading (Table 10). It is important to underline that the reported uncontrolled ammo- nia emissions refer to uncovered storage tank and traditional land-spreading techniques, thus they could be strongly reduced by applying Best Available Techniques (European IPPC Bureau, 2003).

Table 10 also reports emissions of N20 from storage of fresh manure, indirect N20 emissions due to volatilised nitrogen and NOx emissions. We do not have experimental information about the effect of increased nitrogen content due to co-digestion of manure and energy crops but, according to the approach followed by inter- national emission factors (N20 releases are likely to be proportional to nitrogen content), a further emissive increase for N20 could be expected in this case, so reported N20 emissions could be even underestimated.

Nonetheless, spreading of digestate on cropland corresponds to the substitution of chemical fertilisers in quantities that depend on the characteristics of the digestate itself and a coefficient of utilisa- tion of its NPK content. Substitution of chemical fertilisers avoids production and transport-related impacts and also bio-chemical emissions due to their use.

As far as N-fertiliser is concerned, once N-based emission from storage and spreading are accounted for (Table 10), it is assumed that 75% (Masoni and Pampana, 2003; Williams et al., 2006) of the nitrogen remaining after storage can substitute ammonium nitrate. Similarly, P2O5 and K20 content in fresh manure were assumed to be, respectively, 3 kg/t and 8 kg/t. The coefficients of substitution of P2O5 and K2O in terms of single superphosphate and potassium chloride were assumed to be 75% of the nutrient content in the digestate (Jury et al., 2010).

15: It was observed (Table 8) that an increasing quantity of crop silage in the mixture corresponds to a higher quantity of generated electricity/heat per unit of feedstock. Although this was not unex- pected, it is important to underline it in order to correctly interpret the LCA results. In fact, this changes the overall performance of the system under study, i.e. the quantity of final product that will be used as a reference term in order to allocate the overall impacts. Under the hypothesis that the main function of the system is the generation of renewable electricity/heat, the FU is in fact the unit of output energy, while the environmental impacts associated with the cultivation of crops are usually referred to the unit of cultivated land and/or to the mass of harvested feedstock.

Bearing this in mind, the main results of the LCA are shown in Table 11, which highlights two lifecycle energy performance indi- cators (GER and NRE) and four environmental impact indicators (GWP, AP, EP and POCP), as well as land occupancy (LO), volatile solids content (VS) and carbon uptake. All of them are expressed per 1 MJ of net energy delivered (electricity or heat), under the assump- tion of using energy allocation, as reported in Section 3.2. A different allocation (e.g. all impacts to electricity) can be re-calculated using data in Table 8.

With reference to the study area and local current practices, in order to appraise how the uncertainties related to the energy conversion technologies might influence the eco-profile of electric- ity/heat, two columns were used to report the four environmental impact indicators: low and high. Based on emissions from the CHP unit (Table 9), low impact is relevant to the deployment of a SCR reactor and in case of low CH4 slippage, while the high impact col- umn reflects operating conditions without SCR and for high range CH4 slippage.

As a term of reference, the eco-profile of electricity from natu- ral gas taken from the Ecoinvent is also provided, according to the units "Electricity, at cogen 1 MWe lean burn, allocation energy/RER" (low) and "Electricity, natural gas, at power plant/IT" (high).

In a from-cradle-to-grave perspective, it was estimated that 0.2-0.3 MJ of non-renewable energy are used in order to generate MJ of electricity/heat. The most energy efficient bioenergy chain is sorghum/scenario 1, while the less efficient is maize/scenario 3.

Except for POCP, remarkable differences in terms of environ- mental performance were encountered according to the different crops and scenarios. GWP shows the most disperse results: from 42 to 193, CO2 equiv./MJ.

Remarkable differences between low and high impact were found in case of GWP and POCP, thus showing that technological issues related to emissions from the CHP unit have relevance over the whole bioenergy chain.

Shifting from scenario 1 to scenario 3, thus for an increasing crop content, the environmental impacts show a decreasing trend, which can be ascribed to the higher increase in energy output. On the contrary, land occupation increases.

Miscanthus is the energy crop with best environmental perfor- mance for GWP, AP and EP, but is the worst for POCP and LO. This result is in accordance with the findings of Cherubini et al. (2009), where miscanthus showed the highest GHG saving potential.

The comparison between GHG emissions of the bioenergy chains against natural gas shows controversial results, highlighting that renewable energy is not always preferable to non-renewable energy, while for AP, EP and POCP the lower performance of bioen- ergy against natural gas is clearly visible. These results would be helpful to address the discussion on criteria for selecting the non-renewable energy to be substituted by bioenergy in case of consequential LCA modelling (Cherubini and Stromman, 2011).

The analysis of Table 11 highlights a complex and inter- dependent framework that deserves more attention through a contribution analysis of the subsystems in the bioenergy chain. Such a contribution analysis is shown in Figs. 2-5, where plain coloured bars show the results under the hypothesis of lower range impacts during energy conversion (SCR reactor and low CH4 slip- page, see Table 9). The upward error bars show the results under the hypothesis of higher range impacts (without SCR and high CH4 slip- page, see Table 9). The distance between the top of plain coloured bars and the downward error bars highlights the impacts caused by the assumed 5% CH4 leakage at the biogas collection and distribu- tion facility. Environmental impacts for natural gas are also shown as term of reference: plain coloured bars are for co-generation, while upper error bars are for a conventional power plant.
An overview of Figs. 2-5 can be helpful to understand that there is not a single subsystem which overshadows the others, but rather
Table 11 Energy and environmental balance of the bioenergy chain (data per 1 MJ electricity).
a (Low = SCR and low CH4 slippage; High = no SCR and high CH4 slippage). b (Low = CHP; High = conventional).
Fig. 2. Contribution analysis of the impacts/savings in the bioenergy chain: focus on Global Warming (Ma1 = Maize scenario 1; Mi1 = Miscanthus scenario 1).
Fig. 4. Contribution analysis of the impacts/savings in the bioenergy chain: focus on Eutrophication (Ma1 = Maize scenario 1; Mi1 = Miscanthus scenario 1).
Fig. 3. Contribution analysis of the impacts/savings in the bioenergy chain: focus on Acidification (Ma1 = Maize scenario 1; Mi1 = Miscanthus scenario 1).

In fact, this changes the overall performance of the system under study, i.e. the quantity of final product that will be used as a reference term in order to allocate the overall impacts. Under the hypothesis that the main function of the system is the generation of renewable electricity/heat, the FU is in fact the unit of output energy, while the environmental impacts associated with the cultivation of crops are usually referred to the unit of cultivated land and/or to the mass of harvested feedstock.

Bearing this in mind, the main results of the LCA are shown in Table 11, which highlights two lifecycle energy performance indi- cators (GER and NRE) and four environmental impact indicators (GWP, AP, EP and POCP), as well as land occupancy (LO), volatile solids content (VS) and carbon uptake. All of them are expressed per 1 MJ of net energy delivered (electricity or heat), under the assump- tion of using energy allocation, as reported in Section 3.2. A different allocation (e.g. all impacts to electricity) can be re-calculated using data in Table 8.

With reference to the study area and local current practices, in order to appraise how the uncertainties related to the energy conversion technologies might influence the eco-profile of electric- ity/heat, two columns were used to report the four environmental impact indicators: low and high. Based on emissions from the CHP unit (Table 9), low impact is relevant to the deployment of a SCR reactor and in case of low CH4 slippage, while the high impact col- umn reflects operating conditions without SCR and for high range CH4 slippage.

As a term of reference, the eco-profile of electricity from natu- ral gas taken from the Ecoinvent is also provided, according to the units "Electricity, at cogen 1 MWe lean burn, allocation energy/RER" (low) and "Electricity, natural gas, at power plant/IT" (high).

In a from-cradle-to-grave perspective, it was estimated that 0.2-0.3 MJ of non-renewable energy are used in order to generate MJ of electricity/heat. The most energy efficient bioenergy chain is sorghum/scenario 1, while the less efficient is maize/scenario 3.

Except for POCP, remarkable differences in terms of environ- mental performance were encountered according to the different crops and scenarios. GWP shows the most disperse results: from 42 to 193, CO2 equiv./MJ.

Remarkable differences between low and high impact were found in case of GWP and POCP, thus showing that technological issues related to emissions from the CHP unit have relevance over the whole bioenergy chain.

Shifting from scenario 1 to scenario 3, thus for an increasing crop content, the environmental impacts show a decreasing trend, which can be ascribed to the higher increase in energy output. On the contrary, land occupation increases.

Miscanthus is the energy crop with best environmental perfor- mance for GWP, AP and EP, but is the worst for POCP and LO. This result is in accordance with the findings of Cherubini et al. (2009), where miscanthus showed the highest GHG saving potential.

The comparison between GHG emissions of the bioenergy chains against natural gas shows controversial results, highlighting that renewable energy is not always preferable to non-renewable energy, while for AP, EP and POCP the lower performance of bioen- ergy against natural gas is clearly visible. These results would be helpful to address the discussion on criteria for selecting the non-renewable energy to be substituted by bioenergy in case of consequential LCA modelling (Cherubini and Stromman, 2011).

The analysis of Table 11 highlights a complex and inter- dependent framework that deserves more attention through a contribution analysis of the subsystems in the bioenergy chain. Such a contribution analysis is shown in Figs. 2-5, where plain coloured bars show the results under the hypothesis of lower range impacts during energy conversion (SCR reactor and low CH4 slip- page, see Table 9). The upward error bars show the results under the hypothesis of higher range impacts (without SCR and high CH4 slip- page, see Table 9). The distance between the top of plain coloured bars and the downward error bars highlights the impacts caused by the assumed 5% CH4 leakage at the biogas collection and distribu- tion facility. Environmental impacts for natural gas are also shown as term of reference: plain coloured bars are for co-generation, while upper error bars are for a conventional power plant.
An overview of Figs. 2-5 can be helpful to understand that there is not a single subsystem which overshadows the others, but rather
Table 11 Energy and environmental balance of the bioenergy chain (data per 1 MJ electricity).
a (Low = SCR and low CH4 slippage; High = no SCR and high CH4 slippage). b (Low = CHP; High = conventional).
Fig. 2. Contribution analysis of the impacts/savings in the bioenergy chain: focus on Global Warming (Ma1 = Maize scenario 1; Mi1 = Miscanthus scenario 1).
Fig. 4. Contribution analysis of the impacts/savings in the bioenergy chain: focus on Eutrophication (Ma1 = Maize scenario 1; Mi1 = Miscanthus scenario 1).
Fig. 3. Contribution analysis of the impacts/savings in the bioenergy chain: focus on Acidification (Ma1 = Maize scenario 1; Mi1 = Miscanthus scenario 1).
Fig. 5. Contribution analysis of the impacts/savings in the bioenergy chain: focus on Photochemical Smog (Ma1 = Maize scenario 1; Mi1 = Miscanthus scenario 1).

Table 12 Selected atmospheric emissions in the bioenergy chain (data per 1 MJ electricity).

a PMprim. primary particulate matter. b PMtot primary + secondary particulate matter (secondary particulate formation factors: NOx 0.88; SOx 0.54; NH3 0.64; VOC 0.02). C emission at Local scale (i.e. emission from foreground system). d R emission at Regional scale (i.e. emission from foreground system emissions from background system, i.e. from expanded system boundaries).

the life cycle impacts are caused by the mutual contribution of several equally (or almost equally) important elements. Moreover, it can be seen that the relative weight of life cycle subsystems is changing for the different impact categories.

As far as GWP is concerned, the most important subsystems are carbon uptake (biomass growth) and energy conversion, which nearly compensate each other. However, also storage and spread- ing of the digestate have an important contribution and the cultivation phase and avoided fertilisers are not negligible. More- over, technology-related uncertainties and the biogas leakages (highlighted by the error bars) heavily influence the final results.

Coming to AP and EP, the most important contribution is that related to storage and spreading, in particular due to the emission of ammonia. Therefore, it becomes clear that, also in a lifecycle per- spective, control and mitigation of ammonia emissions are of the utmost importance.
Energy conversion is the most important subsystem in case of POCP, followed by storage and spreading.

As far as the specific impact indicators mentioned in Section 3.2, dealing with air pollutants that can be considered precursors of PM10 and PM 2.5, that are mainly nitrogen oxides and ammonia released by the biogas engine and digestate storage and land- spreading, Table 12 reports all the relevant emissions according to different scenarios, and paying attention to the local and regional scales. Figures refer to a case where no SCR is applied, storage tank is not covered and land spreading of digested materials is carried out according to traditional practices (broadcast spreader), which is the typical configuration in the study area. It can easily be observed that the impact in terms of particulates equivalent emissions com- ing from current biogas facilities could be very large (950-1350 mg PM/MJ), in particular when it is compared to modern natural gas power plants (up to 260 mg PM/MJ).

As a matter of fact, the described, strongly impacting, emissions from the analysed renewable energy chains should be drastically minimised by the application of SCR (NOx abatement efficiency up to 90%), covered storage tank, ammonia abatement devices and Best Available Techniques (European IPPC Bureau, 2003) to the land

spreading of digestate (band spreader, trailing shoe, injection incor- poration); the mentioned techniques could reduce the secondary PM emissions to 300 mg PM/MJ, that would be a level comparable to some fossil fuel energy plants (EEA, 2010).

6: <header>6. Conclusions </header>

A detailed LCA of bioenergy production from dedicated crops (maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus) and manure through anaerobic digestion (AD) and combined heat and power CHP gen- eration in Northern Italy was carried out.

The study highlighted that, when addressing environmental sustainability of bioenergy chains, all life cycle phases and subsys- tems must be carefully considered, as there is no single dominating item or aspect in the life cycle impacts, but rather several of them play an important role in the overall sustainability.

The obtained results necessarily reflect the complex combi- nation of the territorial context, including site-specific climate conditions, the local agri-forestry practices, the adopted energy conversion technologies and, finally, the disposal of residues.
Bearing this in mind, these results should not be generalised, but some remarks can certainly be given.

As a general comment, in order to develop meaningful LCAs of bioenergy systems, both input data and inventory results must be fully made available and it should be possible to mathemati- cally manipulate them. Only in this way LCAs of bioenergy can be comparable and can be used as background for further research.
It was confirmed that bioenergy is not automatically synony- mous with sustainable energy, as differences in environmental performance can be remarkable.

In a from-cradle-to-gate perspective, 0.2-0.31 of non- renewable energy are used in order to generate 1 MJ of electricity/heat. This roughly corresponds to a EROI (Energy Return on Investment) index from 3 to 5.

The potential in terms of GHG saving in respect to fossil fuels is encouraging, but the overall performance of the chain depends on several factors and it is possible that, without a life cycle approach,

the final results might disappoint expectations. In this context, the choice of the reference non-renewable energy to be substituted is of primary importance. It was pointed out that the end-of-life of digestate is crucial for AP and EP, especially due to ammonia emissions, but EOL is also important for GWP and POCP. Therefore, mitigation measures are necessary, but paying attention to the fact that the environmental performance of the system as a whole should be verified through a holistic approach, in order to avoid impact shifting. Land use was found to be very variable from crop to crop and from scenario to scenario. Moreover, it was found that the impact in terms of particulates equivalent emissions coming from current biogas facilities could be very large in comparison to modern natural gas power plants, up to 5 times larger, so the effect on air quality could be nega- tive, in particular for compromised areas such as Northern Italy. Anyway, technical solutions that can ensure the compatibility of renewable energy plants do exist but their application is hindered by the formulation of subsidies and the technical legislation that is not suitable to manage the possible environmental impacts. It is expected that the LCA results will be helpful in order to assist public decision-makers during the evaluation of new bioen- ergy projects, also in order to improve the overall environmental performance and boost eco-efficiency. Bearing in mind that bioenergy chains can bring important social and economic benefits that also play a role in the overall sustain- ability, authors suggest incorporation of the life cycle approach in bioenergy policies, energy certification and economic incen- tive schemes, too often lacking in a comprehensive approach that would enhance effectiveness and avoid problem shifting. In such a context, it is also the responsibility of LCA practitioners to work in co-operation with public authorities to ensure that all parties understand LCA studies and put into practice the results. In an ongoing research programme, authors will deepen the aspects of direct and indirect land use change, as well as the use of agri-food waste and by products in substitution of dedicated bioenergy crops. 7: <header>6. Conclusions </header>

A detailed LCA of bioenergy production from dedicated crops (maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus) and manure through anaerobic digestion (AD) and combined heat and power CHP gen- eration in Northern Italy was carried out.

The study highlighted that, when addressing environmental sustainability of bioenergy chains, all life cycle phases and subsys- tems must be carefully considered, as there is no single dominating item or aspect in the life cycle impacts, but rather several of them play an important role in the overall sustainability.

The obtained results necessarily reflect the complex combi- nation of the territorial context, including site-specific climate conditions, the local agri-forestry practices, the adopted energy conversion technologies and, finally, the disposal of residues.
Bearing this in mind, these results should not be generalised, but some remarks can certainly be given.

As a general comment, in order to develop meaningful LCAs of bioenergy systems, both input data and inventory results must be fully made available and it should be possible to mathemati- cally manipulate them. Only in this way LCAs of bioenergy can be comparable and can be used as background for further research.
It was confirmed that bioenergy is not automatically synony- mous with sustainable energy, as differences in environmental performance can be remarkable.

In a from-cradle-to-gate perspective, 0.2-0.31 of non- renewable energy are used in order to generate 1 MJ of electricity/heat. This roughly corresponds to a EROI (Energy Return on Investment) index from 3 to 5.

The potential in terms of GHG saving in respect to fossil fuels is encouraging, but the overall performance of the chain depends on several factors and it is possible that, without a life cycle approach,

the final results might disappoint expectations. In this context, the choice of the reference non-renewable energy to be substituted is of primary importance. It was pointed out that the end-of-life of digestate is crucial for AP and EP, especially due to ammonia emissions, but EOL is also important for GWP and POCP. Therefore, mitigation measures are necessary, but paying attention to the fact that the environmental performance of the system as a whole should be verified through a holistic approach, in order to avoid impact shifting. Land use was found to be very variable from crop to crop and from scenario to scenario. Moreover, it was found that the impact in terms of particulates equivalent emissions coming from current biogas facilities could be very large in comparison to modern natural gas power plants, up to 5 times larger, so the effect on air quality could be nega- tive, in particular for compromised areas such as Northern Italy. Anyway, technical solutions that can ensure the compatibility of renewable energy plants do exist but their application is hindered by the formulation of subsidies and the technical legislation that is not suitable to manage the possible environmental impacts. It is expected that the LCA results will be helpful in order to assist public decision-makers during the evaluation of new bioen- ergy projects, also in order to improve the overall environmental performance and boost eco-efficiency. Bearing in mind that bioenergy chains can bring important social and economic benefits that also play a role in the overall sustain- ability, authors suggest incorporation of the life cycle approach in bioenergy policies, energy certification and economic incen- tive schemes, too often lacking in a comprehensive approach that would enhance effectiveness and avoid problem shifting. In such a context, it is also the responsibility of LCA practitioners to work in co-operation with public authorities to ensure that all parties understand LCA studies and put into practice the results. In an ongoing research programme, authors will deepen the aspects of direct and indirect land use change, as well as the use of agri-food waste and by products in substitution of dedicated bioenergy crops. 9: <header>6. Conclusions </header>

A detailed LCA of bioenergy production from dedicated crops (maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus) and manure through anaerobic digestion (AD) and combined heat and power CHP gen- eration in Northern Italy was carried out.

The study highlighted that, when addressing environmental sustainability of bioenergy chains, all life cycle phases and subsys- tems must be carefully considered, as there is no single dominating item or aspect in the life cycle impacts, but rather several of them play an important role in the overall sustainability.

The obtained results necessarily reflect the complex combi- nation of the territorial context, including site-specific climate conditions, the local agri-forestry practices, the adopted energy conversion technologies and, finally, the disposal of residues.
Bearing this in mind, these results should not be generalised, but some remarks can certainly be given.

As a general comment, in order to develop meaningful LCAs of bioenergy systems, both input data and inventory results must be fully made available and it should be possible to mathemati- cally manipulate them. Only in this way LCAs of bioenergy can be comparable and can be used as background for further research.
It was confirmed that bioenergy is not automatically synony- mous with sustainable energy, as differences in environmental performance can be remarkable.

In a from-cradle-to-gate perspective, 0.2-0.31 of non- renewable energy are used in order to generate 1 MJ of electricity/heat. This roughly corresponds to a EROI (Energy Return on Investment) index from 3 to 5.

The potential in terms of GHG saving in respect to fossil fuels is encouraging, but the overall performance of the chain depends on several factors and it is possible that, without a life cycle approach,

the final results might disappoint expectations. In this context, the choice of the reference non-renewable energy to be substituted is of primary importance. It was pointed out that the end-of-life of digestate is crucial for AP and EP, especially due to ammonia emissions, but EOL is also important for GWP and POCP. Therefore, mitigation measures are necessary, but paying attention to the fact that the environmental performance of the system as a whole should be verified through a holistic approach, in order to avoid impact shifting. Land use was found to be very variable from crop to crop and from scenario to scenario. Moreover, it was found that the impact in terms of particulates equivalent emissions coming from current biogas facilities could be very large in comparison to modern natural gas power plants, up to 5 times larger, so the effect on air quality could be nega- tive, in particular for compromised areas such as Northern Italy. Anyway, technical solutions that can ensure the compatibility of renewable energy plants do exist but their application is hindered by the formulation of subsidies and the technical legislation that is not suitable to manage the possible environmental impacts. It is expected that the LCA results will be helpful in order to assist public decision-makers during the evaluation of new bioen- ergy projects, also in order to improve the overall environmental performance and boost eco-efficiency. Bearing in mind that bioenergy chains can bring important social and economic benefits that also play a role in the overall sustain- ability, authors suggest incorporation of the life cycle approach in bioenergy policies, energy certification and economic incen- tive schemes, too often lacking in a comprehensive approach that would enhance effectiveness and avoid problem shifting. In such a context, it is also the responsibility of LCA practitioners to work in co-operation with public authorities to ensure that all parties understand LCA studies and put into practice the results. In an ongoing research programme, authors will deepen the aspects of direct and indirect land use change, as well as the use of agri-food waste and by products in substitution of dedicated bioenergy crops.
