11: <header>6. Conclusions </header>

A detailed LCA of bioenergy production from dedicated crops (maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus) and manure through anaerobic digestion (AD) and combined heat and power CHP gen- eration in Northern Italy was carried out.

The study highlighted that, when addressing environmental sustainability of bioenergy chains, all life cycle phases and subsys- tems must be carefully considered, as there is no single dominating item or aspect in the life cycle impacts, but rather several of them play an important role in the overall sustainability.

The obtained results necessarily reflect the complex combi- nation of the territorial context, including site-specific climate conditions, the local agri-forestry practices, the adopted energy conversion technologies and, finally, the disposal of residues.
Bearing this in mind, these results should not be generalised, but some remarks can certainly be given.

As a general comment, in order to develop meaningful LCAs of bioenergy systems, both input data and inventory results must be fully made available and it should be possible to mathemati- cally manipulate them. Only in this way LCAs of bioenergy can be comparable and can be used as background for further research.
It was confirmed that bioenergy is not automatically synony- mous with sustainable energy, as differences in environmental performance can be remarkable.

In a from-cradle-to-gate perspective, 0.2-0.31 of non- renewable energy are used in order to generate 1 MJ of electricity/heat. This roughly corresponds to a EROI (Energy Return on Investment) index from 3 to 5.

The potential in terms of GHG saving in respect to fossil fuels is encouraging, but the overall performance of the chain depends on several factors and it is possible that, without a life cycle approach,

the final results might disappoint expectations. In this context, the choice of the reference non-renewable energy to be substituted is of primary importance. It was pointed out that the end-of-life of digestate is crucial for AP and EP, especially due to ammonia emissions, but EOL is also important for GWP and POCP. Therefore, mitigation measures are necessary, but paying attention to the fact that the environmental performance of the system as a whole should be verified through a holistic approach, in order to avoid impact shifting. Land use was found to be very variable from crop to crop and from scenario to scenario. Moreover, it was found that the impact in terms of particulates equivalent emissions coming from current biogas facilities could be very large in comparison to modern natural gas power plants, up to 5 times larger, so the effect on air quality could be nega- tive, in particular for compromised areas such as Northern Italy. Anyway, technical solutions that can ensure the compatibility of renewable energy plants do exist but their application is hindered by the formulation of subsidies and the technical legislation that is not suitable to manage the possible environmental impacts. It is expected that the LCA results will be helpful in order to assist public decision-makers during the evaluation of new bioen- ergy projects, also in order to improve the overall environmental performance and boost eco-efficiency. Bearing in mind that bioenergy chains can bring important social and economic benefits that also play a role in the overall sustain- ability, authors suggest incorporation of the life cycle approach in bioenergy policies, energy certification and economic incen- tive schemes, too often lacking in a comprehensive approach that would enhance effectiveness and avoid problem shifting. In such a context, it is also the responsibility of LCA practitioners to work in co-operation with public authorities to ensure that all parties understand LCA studies and put into practice the results. In an ongoing research programme, authors will deepen the aspects of direct and indirect land use change, as well as the use of agri-food waste and by products in substitution of dedicated bioenergy crops. 13: <header>6. Conclusions </header>

A detailed LCA of bioenergy production from dedicated crops (maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus) and manure through anaerobic digestion (AD) and combined heat and power CHP gen- eration in Northern Italy was carried out.

The study highlighted that, when addressing environmental sustainability of bioenergy chains, all life cycle phases and subsys- tems must be carefully considered, as there is no single dominating item or aspect in the life cycle impacts, but rather several of them play an important role in the overall sustainability.

The obtained results necessarily reflect the complex combi- nation of the territorial context, including site-specific climate conditions, the local agri-forestry practices, the adopted energy conversion technologies and, finally, the disposal of residues.
Bearing this in mind, these results should not be generalised, but some remarks can certainly be given.

As a general comment, in order to develop meaningful LCAs of bioenergy systems, both input data and inventory results must be fully made available and it should be possible to mathemati- cally manipulate them. Only in this way LCAs of bioenergy can be comparable and can be used as background for further research.
It was confirmed that bioenergy is not automatically synony- mous with sustainable energy, as differences in environmental performance can be remarkable.

In a from-cradle-to-gate perspective, 0.2-0.31 of non- renewable energy are used in order to generate 1 MJ of electricity/heat. This roughly corresponds to a EROI (Energy Return on Investment) index from 3 to 5.

The potential in terms of GHG saving in respect to fossil fuels is encouraging, but the overall performance of the chain depends on several factors and it is possible that, without a life cycle approach,

the final results might disappoint expectations. In this context, the choice of the reference non-renewable energy to be substituted is of primary importance. It was pointed out that the end-of-life of digestate is crucial for AP and EP, especially due to ammonia emissions, but EOL is also important for GWP and POCP. Therefore, mitigation measures are necessary, but paying attention to the fact that the environmental performance of the system as a whole should be verified through a holistic approach, in order to avoid impact shifting. Land use was found to be very variable from crop to crop and from scenario to scenario. Moreover, it was found that the impact in terms of particulates equivalent emissions coming from current biogas facilities could be very large in comparison to modern natural gas power plants, up to 5 times larger, so the effect on air quality could be nega- tive, in particular for compromised areas such as Northern Italy. Anyway, technical solutions that can ensure the compatibility of renewable energy plants do exist but their application is hindered by the formulation of subsidies and the technical legislation that is not suitable to manage the possible environmental impacts. It is expected that the LCA results will be helpful in order to assist public decision-makers during the evaluation of new bioen- ergy projects, also in order to improve the overall environmental performance and boost eco-efficiency. Bearing in mind that bioenergy chains can bring important social and economic benefits that also play a role in the overall sustain- ability, authors suggest incorporation of the life cycle approach in bioenergy policies, energy certification and economic incen- tive schemes, too often lacking in a comprehensive approach that would enhance effectiveness and avoid problem shifting. In such a context, it is also the responsibility of LCA practitioners to work in co-operation with public authorities to ensure that all parties understand LCA studies and put into practice the results. In an ongoing research programme, authors will deepen the aspects of direct and indirect land use change, as well as the use of agri-food waste and by products in substitution of dedicated bioenergy crops. 15: A detailed LCA of bioenergy production from dedicated crops (maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus) and manure through anaerobic digestion (AD) and combined heat and power CHP gen- eration in Northern Italy was carried out.

The study highlighted that, when addressing environmental sustainability of bioenergy chains, all life cycle phases and subsys- tems must be carefully considered, as there is no single dominating item or aspect in the life cycle impacts, but rather several of them play an important role in the overall sustainability.

The obtained results necessarily reflect the complex combi- nation of the territorial context, including site-specific climate conditions, the local agri-forestry practices, the adopted energy conversion technologies and, finally, the disposal of residues.
Bearing this in mind, these results should not be generalised, but some remarks can certainly be given.

As a general comment, in order to develop meaningful LCAs of bioenergy systems, both input data and inventory results must be fully made available and it should be possible to mathemati- cally manipulate them. Only in this way LCAs of bioenergy can be comparable and can be used as background for further research.
It was confirmed that bioenergy is not automatically synony- mous with sustainable energy, as differences in environmental performance can be remarkable.

In a from-cradle-to-gate perspective, 0.2-0.31 of non- renewable energy are used in order to generate 1 MJ of electricity/heat. This roughly corresponds to a EROI (Energy Return on Investment) index from 3 to 5.

The potential in terms of GHG saving in respect to fossil fuels is encouraging, but the overall performance of the chain depends on several factors and it is possible that, without a life cycle approach,

the final results might disappoint expectations. In this context, the choice of the reference non-renewable energy to be substituted is of primary importance. It was pointed out that the end-of-life of digestate is crucial for AP and EP, especially due to ammonia emissions, but EOL is also important for GWP and POCP. Therefore, mitigation measures are necessary, but paying attention to the fact that the environmental performance of the system as a whole should be verified through a holistic approach, in order to avoid impact shifting. Land use was found to be very variable from crop to crop and from scenario to scenario. Moreover, it was found that the impact in terms of particulates equivalent emissions coming from current biogas facilities could be very large in comparison to modern natural gas power plants, up to 5 times larger, so the effect on air quality could be nega- tive, in particular for compromised areas such as Northern Italy. Anyway, technical solutions that can ensure the compatibility of renewable energy plants do exist but their application is hindered by the formulation of subsidies and the technical legislation that is not suitable to manage the possible environmental impacts. It is expected that the LCA results will be helpful in order to assist public decision-makers during the evaluation of new bioen- ergy projects, also in order to improve the overall environmental performance and boost eco-efficiency. Bearing in mind that bioenergy chains can bring important social and economic benefits that also play a role in the overall sustain- ability, authors suggest incorporation of the life cycle approach in bioenergy policies, energy certification and economic incen- tive schemes, too often lacking in a comprehensive approach that would enhance effectiveness and avoid problem shifting. In such a context, it is also the responsibility of LCA practitioners to work in co-operation with public authorities to ensure that all parties understand LCA studies and put into practice the results. In an ongoing research programme, authors will deepen the aspects of direct and indirect land use change, as well as the use of agri-food waste and by products in substitution of dedicated bioenergy crops. 17: A detailed LCA of bioenergy production from dedicated crops (maize, sorghum, triticale and miscanthus) and manure through anaerobic digestion (AD) and combined heat and power CHP gen- eration in Northern Italy was carried out.

The study highlighted that, when addressing environmental sustainability of bioenergy chains, all life cycle phases and subsys- tems must be carefully considered, as there is no single dominating item or aspect in the life cycle impacts, but rather several of them play an important role in the overall sustainability.

The obtained results necessarily reflect the complex combi- nation of the territorial context, including site-specific climate conditions, the local agri-forestry practices, the adopted energy conversion technologies and, finally, the disposal of residues.
Bearing this in mind, these results should not be generalised, but some remarks can certainly be given.

As a general comment, in order to develop meaningful LCAs of bioenergy systems, both input data and inventory results must be fully made available and it should be possible to mathemati- cally manipulate them. Only in this way LCAs of bioenergy can be comparable and can be used as background for further research.
It was confirmed that bioenergy is not automatically synony- mous with sustainable energy, as differences in environmental performance can be remarkable.

In a from-cradle-to-gate perspective, 0.2-0.31 of non- renewable energy are used in order to generate 1 MJ of electricity/heat. This roughly corresponds to a EROI (Energy Return on Investment) index from 3 to 5.

The potential in terms of GHG saving in respect to fossil fuels is encouraging, but the overall performance of the chain depends on several factors and it is possible that, without a life cycle approach,

the final results might disappoint expectations. In this context, the choice of the reference non-renewable energy to be substituted is of primary importance. It was pointed out that the end-of-life of digestate is crucial for AP and EP, especially due to ammonia emissions, but EOL is also important for GWP and POCP. Therefore, mitigation measures are necessary, but paying attention to the fact that the environmental performance of the system as a whole should be verified through a holistic approach, in order to avoid impact shifting. Land use was found to be very variable from crop to crop and from scenario to scenario. Moreover, it was found that the impact in terms of particulates equivalent emissions coming from current biogas facilities could be very large in comparison to modern natural gas power plants, up to 5 times larger, so the effect on air quality could be nega- tive, in particular for compromised areas such as Northern Italy. Anyway, technical solutions that can ensure the compatibility of renewable energy plants do exist but their application is hindered by the formulation of subsidies and the technical legislation that is not suitable to manage the possible environmental impacts. It is expected that the LCA results will be helpful in order to assist public decision-makers during the evaluation of new bioen- ergy projects, also in order to improve the overall environmental performance and boost eco-efficiency. Bearing in mind that bioenergy chains can bring important social and economic benefits that also play a role in the overall sustain- ability, authors suggest incorporation of the life cycle approach in bioenergy policies, energy certification and economic incen- tive schemes, too often lacking in a comprehensive approach that would enhance effectiveness and avoid problem shifting. In such a context, it is also the responsibility of LCA practitioners to work in co-operation with public authorities to ensure that all parties understand LCA studies and put into practice the results. In an ongoing research programme, authors will deepen the aspects of direct and indirect land use change, as well as the use of agri-food waste and by products in substitution of dedicated bioenergy crops.
